nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or standing waters of the rio Guaporé drainage (known as the rio Iténez in Bolivia); sandy and leaf-littered substrates in clearwater and blackwater streams over the Brazilian Shield

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (mandatory — protects delicate barbels)
leaf litter (dried oak or Indian almond leaves)

diet.notes: Opportunistic benthic omnivore; accepts high-quality sinking pellets, micro-wafers, and algae wafers as staples. Supplement 2–3 times per week with small live or frozen foods — bloodworms, white worms, Tubifex, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Wild diet includes benthic invertebrates, detritus, plant matter, and insect larvae. Feed at or after lights-out when possible; this species is most active at dusk and dawn.

goodWith: other peaceful small tetras (neons, ember tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) with compatible water parameters
other corydoras species with similar water requirements
dwarf freshwater shrimp (larger species; fry may be eaten)
hatchetfish and other upper-level community species
avoidWith: salt-requiring species (corydoras are highly sensitive to salt)
fast, boisterous species that outcompete for sinking food

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Hoplisoma caudimaculatum (Corydoras caudimaculatus); shows characteristic caudal-peduncle spot. Verify license before use.

summary: A compact, easy-care corydoras endemic to the rio Guaporé drainage of Brazil and Bolivia, instantly recognised by its bold black spot at the caudal peduncle base. Peaceful, shoaling bottom-dweller; ideal for small community tanks with soft sand and subdued lighting. A 2024 phylogenomic revision (Dias et al.) transfers this species to the resurrected genus Hoplisoma as H. caudimaculatum.
careNotes:
The Tailspot Corydoras is one of the smaller, more manageable members of the Callichthyidae family, reaching approximately 4–5 cm standard length. A group of at least six individuals is strongly recommended — these are gregarious shoaling fish that become noticeably stressed and reclusive when kept in smaller numbers. A 60-litre aquarium with a 60 cm footprint is a practical minimum for a group of six, though larger tanks allow a more natural shoaling dynamic.

Substrate choice is critical: fine, smooth sand is mandatory. Coarse or sharp gravel abrades the sensitive barbels, leading to infection and permanent damage. A sand depth of at least 3 cm allows natural sifting behaviour. Partial water changes of 25–30% weekly are important, as this species is particularly sensitive to elevated nitrate concentrations; clean, well-oxygenated water is essential.

Light levels should be kept moderate to low. The addition of dried leaf litter (Indian almond or oak leaves) and driftwood replicates the species’ natural blackwater biotope and provides cover as well as tannins that buffer pH gently downward. Plants such as Anubias and Java fern are excellent companions.

Because corydoras are facultative air-breathers — periodically dashing to the surface to gulp atmospheric air via intestinal respiration — a small gap between the water surface and the tank lid must always be maintained. This behaviour is normal and should not be mistaken for oxygen deficiency.

Caution: the hardened pectoral-fin spines can pierce skin and may carry a mild venom; always use a plastic container to transfer these fish, never a net (spines entangle in mesh). Salt must be strictly avoided — this species has no tolerance for aquarium salt or brackish conditions.

Taxonomic note: a 2024 phylogenomic study by Dias et al. (Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society) resurrected the genus Hoplisoma, transferring this species to Hoplisoma caudimaculatum. The trade name Corydoras caudimaculatus remains in widespread use and is retained here as the primary scientific name pending broader adoption.
breedingNotes:
Moderately straightforward to breed using methods common to most corydoras. Condition the group with varied live and frozen foods over several weeks. Trigger spawning by performing a large (50–70%) water change with water that is several degrees cooler than the display tank, while simultaneously increasing surface agitation and oxygenation — this mimics the onset of the rainy season. A 2:1 male-to-female ratio is recommended.

Females carry 2–4 adhesive eggs cupped between the pelvic fins (the characteristic “T-position” spawning embrace); eggs are then pressed onto clean, smooth surfaces — plant leaves, broad-leaved plants, or the aquarium glass. A single spawning session produces roughly 80–120 eggs in total. Incubation takes approximately 3–4 days at 23–25 °C. Once fry have absorbed their yolk sacs (a further 2–3 days), they accept microworms, Artemia nauplii, and finely powdered fry food. Remove eggs or fry to a separate rearing tank for best survival rates, as adults and other inhabitants will consume them.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras caudimaculatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (45–50 mm SL), temperature (20–26 °C), pH (6.0–7.0), hardness (36–179 ppm / approx. 2–10 dH), native range (rio Guaporé drainage, western Brazil and northeastern Bolivia), habitat, substrate requirements, breeding protocol (2:1 M:F ratio, cool-water trigger, 3–4 day incubation), behaviour, and tankmate guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Hoplisoma caudimaculatum
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes, subfamily Corydoradinae), maximum SL 4.2 cm, temperature 22–26 °C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 2–25 dH, demersal freshwater habitat, trophic level 3.0, facultative air-breathing, IUCN Least Concern (2020). Current accepted name listed as Hoplisoma caudimaculatum per Dias et al. 2024 revision.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cory Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General corydoras care reference; confirmed minimum group of six, smooth sand substrate requirement, omnivorous diet (sinking pellets, live/frozen bloodworms, blackworms), breeding trigger via cooler water changes, temperature range 72–82 °F (22–28 °C). No page specific to C. caudimaculatus; general Corydoras guidance applied.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Tailspot Corydoras Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4–5 cm), temperature (22–27 °C), pH (6.3–7.3), hardness (4–17 °N), lifespan (3–5 years), minimum group of five, sinking-food diet with frozen supplement, sensitivity to elevated nitrates, and facultative air-breathing behaviour.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Corydoras revised: new genus names (Dias et al. 2024)
additional.notes: Confirmed the 2024 Dias et al. phylogenomic revision published in the Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society; Hoplisoma resurrected as the correct genus for C. caudimaculatus (now Hoplisoma caudimaculatum); Hoplisoma is the largest resurrected genus with 87+ described species.

fish.breedingNotes: Bred using standard corydoras protocol: condition with live/frozen foods, trigger with 50–70% cool-water change and increased oxygenation. Use 2:1 M:F ratio. Females carry 2–4 eggs between pelvic fins before depositing on smooth surfaces. Approx. 80–120 eggs per session; hatch in 3–4 days. Fry accept microworms and Artemia nauplii after yolk absorption. Remove eggs to separate tank for best fry survival.
